statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, find the next lexicographically greater permutation. If no such permutation exists, then rearrange the array to its lexicographically smallest permutation. The array consists of distinct integers from 1 to n where n is the length of the array. The next permutation should be in-place, meaning it should modify the input array to be the next permutation if one exists.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer n, the length of the array. The second line contains n distinct integers from 1 to n, representing the array.
statement.outputFormat: The modified array representing the next lexicographically greater permutation if one exists, otherwise the lexicographically smallest permutation.

constraints.description: Array of distinct integers from 1 to n

examples.explanation: The next lexicographically greater permutation of [1, 2, 3] is [1, 3, 2].
Since [3, 2, 1] is the last permutation, the next permutation is the first permutation, which is [1, 2, 3].
The next lexicographically greater permutation of [1, 2] is [2, 1].

hints.text: the problem requires finding the next lexicographically greater permutation of the given array. start by considering how permutations are ordered lexicographically.

to find the next greater permutation, consider the rightmost decreasing subsequence in the current permutation. if the subsequence is of length 1, the next permutation is the lexicographically smallest permutation.

to find the next greater permutation, consider the rightmost decreasing subsequence in the current permutation. if the subsequence is of length 1, the next permutation is the lexicographically smallest permutation. otherwise, find the largest index k such that arr[k] < arr[k + 1].

to find the next greater permutation, consider the rightmost decreasing subsequence in the current permutation. if the subsequence is of length 1, the next permutation is the lexicographically smallest permutation. otherwise, find the largest index k such that arr[k] < arr[k + 1]. then find the largest index l > k such that arr[k] < arr[l].

to find the next greater permutation, consider the rightmost decreasing subsequence in the current permutation. if the subsequence is of length 1, the next permutation is the lexicographically smallest permutation. otherwise, find the largest index k such that arr[k] < arr[k + 1]. then find the largest index l > k such that arr[k] < arr[l]. swap arr[k] and arr[l], and reverse the subsequence from arr[k + 1] to the end.

editorial.title: Next Permutation
editorial.intuition: The problem requires generating the next lexicographically larger permutation of a given array of distinct integers. The brute force approach would involve generating all permutations and then finding the next larger one, but this is inefficient due to its high time complexity.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force method is correct but slow because it involves generating all possible permutations of the array, which results in a time complexity of O(n!), making it impractical for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: An improvement over the brute force approach is to find the first pair of two successive elements from the right that are in increasing order, and then swap the first element with the smallest element to its right that is larger than it.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves iterating through the array from right to left to find the first pair of elements in increasing order, then swapping the first element with the smallest larger element to its right, and finally sorting the elements to the right of the swapped pair. This approach has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(1), making it efficient for large inputs.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that it generates the next lexicographically larger permutation. This is achieved by finding the first pair of elements in increasing order from the right, swapping the first element with the smallest larger element to its right, and then sorting the remaining elements to ensure the resulting permutation is the next larger one.
editorial.dryRun: To validate the solution, a dry run can be performed with sample inputs to verify that the output is indeed the next lexicographically larger permutation.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly identifying the pair of elements to swap
Failing to sort the remaining elements after swapping
Not handling the case where the input array is already the last permutation
Using an inefficient algorithm with high time complexity
Not considering the constraints on the input array

solutions.python:
import sys
it = iter(sys.stdin.read().split())
n = int(next(it))
nums = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
def next_permutation(nums):
    i = len(nums) - 2
    while i >= 0 and nums[i] >= nums[i + 1]:
        i -= 1
    if i < 0:
        nums[:] = sorted(nums)
        return
    j = len(nums) - 1
    while nums[j] <= nums[i]:
        j -= 1
    nums[i], nums[j] = nums[j], nums[i]
    nums[i + 1:] = sorted(nums[i + 1:])
next_permutation(nums)
print(' '.join(map(str, nums)))
